coredata.dc:title: A DFT and QTAIM study of the adsorption of organic molecules over the copper-doped coronene and circumcoronene
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Malček, Michal
Cordeiro, M. Natalia D.S.
coredata.dc:description: Graphene based materials are nowadays extensively studied because of their potential applications as gas sensors, biosensors or adsorbents. Doping the graphene surface with heteroatoms or transition metals can improve its electronic properties and chemical reactivity. Polyaromatic hydrocarbons coronene and circumcoronene can be used as models of tiny graphene quantum dots. The adsorption of a set of organic molecules (water, hydrogen peroxide, hydrogen sulfide, methanol, ethanol and oxygen molecule) over the copper-doped coronene and circumcoronene was theoretically studied using density functional theory (DFT) and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM). In the case of coronene, only one site was considered for the Cu-doping, whereas in the case of circumcoronene being a polyaromatic hydrocarbon composed of 54 carbon atoms, three different sites for Cu-doping were considered. For the systems under study, the adsorption of O2 was found energetically the most favorable, with energetic outcome ranging from –3.1 to −3.7eV related to the position of dopant Cu atom. Changes in the topology of charge densities at Cu and in its vicinity after the adsorption of studied molecules were investigated in the framework of QTAIM. In addition, QTAIM analysis of bond critical points (BCP) was employed to study the character of the newly formed chemical bonds. The results of this study point out the suitability of Cu-doped graphene materials as sensors and/or adsorbents in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Circumcoronene
DFT
Doped graphene
Electronic structure
QTAIM analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption energies, QTAIM charge densities and PDOS
QTAIM analysis of bond critical points

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures and atom labeling of copper-doped coronene (a) and circumcoronene 1 (b). Note that, doped circumcoronenes 2 and 3 contain copper in the positions of C11 and C24, respectively (marked with yellow rings), while the labeling of the rest of the atoms remains the same.
Side-view of the optimized B3LYP/6-311G*Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 showing the elevation of the dopant atom.
Scheme of spin densities of Cu-doped coronene, the isovalue was set to (−)0.005.
Scheme of spin densities of Cu-doped circumcoronenes1 (a), 2 (b) and 3 (c), the isovalue was set to (−)0.005.

Scheme of spin densities of Cu-doped coronene with adsorbed molecule of water (a), hydrogen peroxide (b), hydrogen sulfide (c), methanol (d), ethanol (e) and oxygen (f), the isovalue was set to (−)0.005.

Scheme of spin densities of Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 with adsorbed molecule of water (a), hydrogen peroxide (b), hydrogen sulfide (c), methanol (d), ethanol (e) and oxygen (f), the isovalue was set to (−)0.005.

Scheme of spin densities of Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 with adsorbed molecule of water (a), hydrogen peroxide (b), hydrogen sulfide (c), methanol (d), ethanol (e) and oxygen (f), the isovalue was set to (−)0.005.

Scheme of spin densities of Cu-doped circumcoronene 3 with adsorbed molecule of water (a), hydrogen peroxide (b), hydrogen sulfide (c), methanol (d), ethanol (e) and oxygen (f), the isovalue was set to (−)0.005.
PDOS of free O2 gas-phase molecule and of O2 adsorbed on Cu-doped coronene (a), Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 (b), 2 (c), and 3 (d). The Fermi level is set to zero.

B3LYP/6-311G** BCP characteristics, i.e., charge density ρ BCP, Laplacian Δρ BCP, ellipticity ε BCP and delocalization index DI of Cu-C1 (a), Cu-C2 (b) and Cu-C11 (c) bonds in Cu-doped circumcoronene 1. Particular adsorbents are depicted on the X axis.

B3LYP/6-311G** BCP characteristics, i.e., charge density ρ BCP, Laplacian Δρ BCP, ellipticity ε BCP and delocalization index DI of Cu-C12 (a), Cu-C3 (b) bonds in Cu-doped circumcoronene 2. Particular adsorbents are depicted on the X axis.

B3LYP/6-311G** BCP characteristics, i.e., charge density ρ BCP, Laplacian Δρ BCP, ellipticity ε BCP and delocalization index DI of Cu-C11 (a), Cu-C25 (b) bonds in Cu-doped circumcoronene 3. Particular adsorbents are depicted on the X axis.

B3LYP/6-311G** BCP characteristics, i.e., charge density ρ BCP, Laplacian Δρ BCP, ellipticity ε BCP and delocalization index DI of Cu–O (or Cu–S for H2S) bond in Cu-doped coronene (a), Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 (b), 2 (c), and 3 (d). Particular adsorbents are depicted on the X axis.

B3LYP/6-311G** BCP characteristics, i.e., charge density ρ BCP, Laplacian Δρ BCP, ellipticity ε BCP and delocalization index DI of newly formed C–C bond in Cu-doped coronene (a), Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 (b) and 3 (c). Particular adsorbents are depicted on the X axis.
Scheme of BCPs in coordination complexes Cu(H2O)6 (a) and Cu(H2O)5 (b). Abbreviations in subscripts E and A stand for equatorial and axial, respectively, due to the position of the ligand (water).

B3LYP optimized distances of the Cu-doped systems without and with adsorbed molecules. The last column on the right shows the newly formed C–C bond in the five-membered ring. All values are in Ångströms.
B3LYP optimized bond angles (in degrees) of the studied Cu-doped systems and calculated adsorption energies (in electronvolts).
QTAIM charge densities at Cu and its binding carbon atoms of the optimized Cu-doped systems without and with adsorbed molecules.

B3LYP/6-311G** BCP characteristics, i.e., charge density ρ BCP, Laplacian Δρ BCP, ellipticity ε BCP and delocalization index DI of coordination complexes Cu(H2O)6 and Cu(H2O)5. For the identification of BCPE (equatorial) and BCPA (axial) see Fig. 13.

head.ce:title: A DFT and QTAIM study of the adsorption of organic molecules over the copper-doped coronene and circumcoronene

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: LAQV@REQUIMTE, Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Porto, Rua do Campo Alegre 687, 4169-007 Porto, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: LAQV@REQUIMTE, Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Porto

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene based materials are nowadays extensively studied because of their potential applications as gas sensors, biosensors or adsorbents. Doping the graphene surface with heteroatoms or transition metals can improve its electronic properties and chemical reactivity. Polyaromatic hydrocarbons coronene and circumcoronene can be used as models of tiny graphene quantum dots. The adsorption of a set of organic molecules (water, hydrogen peroxide, hydrogen sulfide, methanol, ethanol and oxygen molecule) over the copper-doped coronene and circumcoronene was theoretically studied using density functional theory (DFT) and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM). In the case of coronene, only one site was considered for the Cu-doping, whereas in the case of circumcoronene being a polyaromatic hydrocarbon composed of 54 carbon atoms, three different sites for Cu-doping were considered. For the systems under study, the adsorption of O2 was found energetically the most favorable, with energetic outcome ranging from –3.1 to −3.7eV related to the position of dopant Cu atom. Changes in the topology of charge densities at Cu and in its vicinity after the adsorption of studied molecules were investigated in the framework of QTAIM. In addition, QTAIM analysis of bond critical points (BCP) was employed to study the character of the newly formed chemical bonds. The results of this study point out the suitability of Cu-doped graphene materials as sensors and/or adsorbents in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene (a two dimensional monolayer of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms) has since its rediscovery and isolation in 2004 [1] attracted a considerable interest because of its still growing number of potential applications [2]. Indeed, due to their extraordinary chemical, mechanical, electronic and optical properties graphene materials have found utilization as detectors of individual gas molecules [3], biological and chemical sensors [4–6] or in energy systems as capacitors and supercapacitors [7]. Schedin et al. have pointed out that graphene can be used as a highly sensitive gas sensor [3]. However, pristine graphene behaves like a zero-band-gap semiconductor, its valence and conduction bands overlapping each other at the Brillouin zone [8]. Doping is able to open this band gap and hence improve the electronic properties and chemical reactivity of the material [8]. Actually, it has been shown that the adsorption ability of graphene towards small organic molecules is often weak and can be significantly strengthened by doping or decorating with different heteroatoms [9–17]. For example, Zhou et al. [9] have investigated in great detail the adsorption of O2, CO, NO2 and NH3 on graphene surfaces (GS) doped with Cu, Ag and Au using ab initio simulations. The authors found that the best improvement of GS reactivity towards the adsorbate was achieved by Au-doping [9]. Recently, adsorption behavior of H2O2 over pristine as well as doped GS have been computationally studied by Düzenli using a large scale of dopant atoms ranging from N, B or S to metallic atoms such as Cu, Ag, Au, Pd or Pt [10]. Therein, the Cu-doped GS has been determined as the most sensitive surface for the reduction of H2O2. Liu and Huang [11] have used Pt over the N-doped GS to get insight into the activation of O2 molecule for oxygen reduction reaction. They suggested that the positive polarization charges in Pt can significantly decrease the O2 dissociation barrier [11]. Mohammadi-Manesh et al. have theoretically investigated the adsorption of H2S on the GS (containing 50 carbon atoms in supercell) decorated with Cu, CuO [12] and Cu2O [13] as possible nanosensors. A significant increase of the electrical conductance has been found for CuO-GS and Cu2O-GS systems after the adsorption of H2S [12,13]. GS comprising 78 carbon atoms and decorated with Ni has been employed to study formaldehyde adsorption by Maaghoul et al. [14] The authors have shown that, even though the energetically most preferable adsorption was for only one formaldehyde molecule, up to four formaldehyde molecules could be possibly adsorbed on one Ni atom [14]. Similar results pointing to the enhanced adsorption ability towards small gas molecules by doping the GS have been reported also by Rad [15]−adsorption of N2O and NO2 on Al-doped graphene, and Wanno and Tabtimsai [16]−adsorption of CO on graphene doped with several transition metals from group VIII B. The latter works represent however, only a limited number of contributions related to this immensely studied topic, more examples can be found e.g. in the works cited in Refs. [9–17].

In this work, the adsorption of small organic molecules on copper-doped coronene and circumcoronene is studied combining the Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Bader's Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM) [18]. Different placements of the copper-dopant within the materials’ backbone are considered. The outline of paper is as follows: Firstly, computational details are presented at Section 2. Subsequently, Section 3 includes the results of this work and it is divided into three parts. Its first subsection is focused on the structural parameters i.e. bond distances and angles and their changes after the adsorption. In the second subsection adsorption energies and the topology of charge and spin density in the framework of QTAIM analysis are discussed, whereas the third subsection is devoted to the QTAIM analysis of bond critical points (BCP). Finally, Section 4 contains the main conclusions from this work.

Two polyaromatic hydrocarbons (PAH), coronene and circumcoronene, were chosen as the model systems. These highly symmetric molecules are composed of 7 and 19 benzene rings, including 24 and 54 carbon atoms, respectively. Especially the larger one, circumcoronene, has been previously used as a model graphene sheet in electronic properties studies because of its sufficient size and the presence of terminating hydrogen atoms [19–22]. Both studied systems have been optimized and subsequently doped with one copper atom by replacing one carbon atom within their backbone. In the case of the coronene there is only one possible position i.e. to replace one of the carbons from the inner layer/circle (see Fig. 1a). On the other hand, the structure of circumcoronene offers three different sites where to place the copper atom, particularly by replacing carbons labeled as C3, C11 and C24. (The labeling of atoms is shown in Fig. 1b.) These three doped circumcoronene systems are denoted as 1, 2 and 3 according to the placement of the copper atom as Cu3, Cu11 and Cu24, respectively. Note that, carbons from the outer layer/circle were not considered for the replacement, because such system does not represent the doping of an infinite GS. From this point of view, the most suitable model system should be the doped circumcoronene 1 shown in Fig. 1b.

Geometry optimizations of coronene and circumcoronene species (pristine as well as Cu-doped) were performed by applying the DFT method B3LYP [23–26] along with the basis sets 6-311G** and 6-311G* [27,28], respectively. The energy-based criterion of the SCF convergence was set to 10–8 Hartree in all systems. The B3LYP is a reliable and high quality density functional method recently used in the study of different similar systems [10,14–16,21,22]. Solvent effects during the geometry optimizations were simulated for water using the polarizable continuum solvent model (PCM) [29,30]. Vibrational analysis was employed to exclude saddle-point configurations (i.e. absence of imaginary vibrations) and to confirm that the optimal geometries correspond to energy minima. In the next step, an organic molecule was placed over the copper atom of every optimized system. The initial distance between the organic molecule and Cu was ca. 2Å. The studied set of adsorbate molecules consisted of water, hydrogen peroxide, hydrogen sulfide, methanol, ethanol and the oxygen molecule. All these systems (Cu-doped PAH plus interacting molecule of adsorbate) were then again optimized at the same level of theory as mentioned above, including vibrational analysis. For comparison, all of the studied systems were also re-optimized using the PBE0 [31,32] functional. All the aforementioned geometry optimization calculations were carried out using Gaussian09 program package [33]. QTAIM analysis [18] was performed with the AIMAll package [34] for every system under study using the checkpoint files obtained from the B3LYP DFT calculations. Density of states (DOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) calculations were carried out using GaussSum package [35]. Visualization of calculated structures and charge densities was performed using Chemcraft [36] and Molekel [37] software suits, respectively. In the case of visualization of spin density, the isovalue was set to (−)0.005.

The interaction (adsorption) energy E
ads between the Cu-doped PAH and the adsorbate molecule was calculated using the equation
(1)
E
ads
=
E
PAH
/
adsorbate
– (
E
PAH
+
E
adsorbate
)
where E
PAH/adsorbate is the energy of Cu-doped PAH with adsorbed molecule, E
PAH is the energy of Cu-doped PAH itself and E
adsorbate is the energy of a particular adsorbate. According to this definition, negative or positive values of E
ads correspond to exothermic or endothermic processes, respectively. Following geometry optimizations, the interactions energies were corrected for the basis set superposition error (BSSE) via the counterpoise scheme of Boys and Bernardi [38].

Naturally, because of the larger number of electronic shells and volume of the copper atom in relation to carbon, the planar structure of coronene and/or circumcoronene is perturbed after the doping. The copper atom is pulled above (or below) the plane of the carbon surface, for illustration see Fig. 2. This fact has been already shown for different types of metal-doped GS, see e.g. works in Refs. [9–11,15–17]. Moreover, the regular six-membered ring structure becomes slightly distorted, especially in the case of Cu-doped circumcoronene 3 containing the copper atom near the outer carbon layer, i.e. in the least stabilized position. Interestingly, it is this system [3] which is energetically the most preferable one among the studied Cu-doped circumcoronenes, as can be seen from the comparison of their DFT energies (see Table S1 in Supporting Information − SI). Similar perturbations in planar C structure have been previously reported for the graphene oxides, i.e. graphene-like sheets decorated by oxygen containing functional groups [39]. Herein, the “edge” position of the functional group was found energetically more favorable than the “center” one [39], which is basically in agreement with our results. The energy band gap of pure circumcoronene (being 2.86eV) decreases after the doping to 1.77eV, 2.15eV and 2.15eV for Cu-doped circumcoronene 1, 2 and 3, respectively. Schematic drawings of all Cu-doped structures are shown in
Figs. 3 and 4, including also their spin densities. The aforementioned structural distortions lead to the prolongation of Cu–C bond lengths up to 1.84–1.89Å in the case of Cu-doped coronene, and to 1.86–1.91Å in the case of Cu-doped circumcoronene. For comparison, Düzenli reported Cu–C bond distances of 1.91Å in the copper doped GS constructed of 73 carbon atoms using B3LYP calculations [10]. Note that, the average optimized bond distance between two carbons in coronene as well as in circumcoronene is ca 1.43Å. The B3LYP obtained bond distances between copper and the neighboring carbon atoms and also between copper and oxygen (or sulfur) from the adsorbed molecules are summarized in Table 1.

One can see from Table 1 that, after the adsorption of any of the studied molecules, the copper atom is pulled stronger from the carbon surface, i.e. the Cu-C distances become larger. In the case of Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronenes 2 and 3, almost all of the adsorbed molecules pull the copper atom so far from its original position that it leads to the transformation of originally six-membered ring into a five-membered one. This transformation implicates a formation of a new C–C bond, the bond lengths of which being also presented in Table 1. Presence of five-membered ring is clearly visible in the pictures of Cu-doped coronene/circumcoronene with the adsorbed molecules in
Figs. 5–8. After this transformation two of the three Cu-C distances are considerably larger than 2Å, that is, as shown in Table 1, the Cu-C4, Cu-C2 values in Cu-doped coronene and Cu-C24, Cu-C3 and Cu-C11, Cu-C23 values in Cu-doped circumcoronenes 2 and 3, respectively. The remaining Cu-C distances (i.e. the Cu-C9, Cu-C12 and Cu-C25 in coronene, circumcoronene 2 and 3, respectively) varies around 1.9Å what more or less corresponds to the bond length of the initial doped system without interacting molecule. From all the adsorbed molecules on doped coronene only the oxygen molecule preserves the original (although distorted) six-membered ring structure (Fig. 5f). Moreover, the Cu-doped circumcoronenes 2 and 3 contain a five-membered ring in the case of all adsorbed molecules (see Figs. 7 and 8, respectively). On the contrary to these findings, the six-membered ring structure is preserved in Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 regardless of the type of adsorbate molecule (Fig. 6). Obviously, in this particular structure Cu is well stabilized by the surrounding carbon atoms with Cu–C bond distances from 1.8 to 2Å. For brevity, a summary of bond angles between Cu and its binding carbons for all studied species, with and without adsorbed molecule, is presented in Table 2. It can be seen from Table 2 that values of all three C-Cu-C angles of Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 systems are around 90° (±5°), while in the all other cases one of the C-Cu-C angles is dramatically lower than the rest two. The measure of this acute C-Cu-C angle is ca. 42° in Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 and ca. 37° in Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronene 3 (see Table 2). The presence of this acute angle goes hand in hand with the transformation of six-membered ring into the five-membered one. The newly formed C–C bond in the five-membered ring is a little longer in comparison to the rest of the C–C bonds. The obtained distances of the new C–C bond are around 1.57Å, 1.65Å and 1.60Å in Cu-doped coronene, circumcoronene 2 and circumcoronene 3, respectively. These values indicate that it is not a standard aromatic C–C bond, as it will be discussed in more detail later on.

Another monitored parameter is the distance between Cu and oxygen or sulfur in the case of H2S from the interacting molecule (see Table 1). Herein, the largest Cu-O distances have been obtained for Cu-doped circumcoronene 1, with almost all values exceeding 2Å. The only exception is the one related to O2 (1.85Å). This results from the fact that the Cu in doped circumcoronene 1 is well captured in the carbon surface and sterically more hindered than in the other studied systems. Excluding system 1, the Cu-O distances in the remaining studied species are shorter, being around 1.95Å (or even 1.85Å or 1.9Å in the case of Cu-doped coronene plus O2 or Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 plus O2, respectively). These Cu-O distances obtained for adsorbed O2 are in qualitative agreement with the one reported by Zhou et al. (1.92Å) [9]. The special case is the adsorption of H2S. Calculated Cu-S distances are from the interval 2.28–2.46Å. The largest value has been found for the Cu-doped circumcoronene 1, due to the same steric hinders affecting the Cu-O distances, while the remaining values related to the Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronenes 2 and 3 are in a quantitative agreement with each other (Table 1). For comparison, Mohammadi-Manesh et al. [12] have reported a Cu-S distance of 2.18Å for the H2S/Cu-graphene system employing the PBE DFT functional. However, it has to be mentioned that in their work Cu has been attached to the unperturbed GS and not replacing one of the carbons [12].

The corresponding bond distances obtained by the PBE0 method are provided in Table S-2 in SI. As can be seen from the comparison of Table 1 and Table S-2, the larger differences between B3LYP and PBE0 values are found only in Cu–C bonds in Cu-doped coronene itself, Cu-doped circumcoronene 3 itself and in Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 + H2O, H2O2 and H2S. In these five particular cases the PBE0 method yields, in contradiction to the B3LYP one, the formation of above mentioned five-membered C ring. In the rest of the studied systems the bond distances obtained either by B3LYP or PBE0 are qualitatively comparable (with differences being less than 7%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption energies, QTAIM charge densities and PDOS
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption energies (E
ads ) of particular systems are presented in Table 2. As can be seen, all the investigated processes are exothermic (i.e. having negative values of E
ads ). The highest adsorption energies have been found in the case of O2 adsorption, varying from −3.07eV (Cu-doped circumcoronene 2) to –3.70 and −3.71eV (Cu-doped coronene and Cu-doped circumcoronene 1, respectively). For comparison, Zhou et al. [9] have reported O2 adsorption energy on Cu-doped graphene of −1.59eV and Liu and Huang [11] have reported O2 adsorption energy of −2.15eV on single Pt-doped GS and −3.43eV on Pt over N-doped GS, both using periodic DFT calculations. On the other hand, the lowest energetic outcomes have been obtained in the case of H2S and H2O2 adsorptions on Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 (E
ads = −0.46eV and −0.55eV, respectively). One can also see from Table 2 that the adsorption energies of H2O, methanol and ethanol are similar to each other when considering the same adsorbent. These E
ads values are around –1.0, –0.8, –1.1 and –1.0 for Cu-doped coronene, circumcoronene 1, 2 and 3, respectively. Taking into account all studied adsorbates (apart from O2) the higher adsorption energies have been obtained in the case of Cu-doped circumcoronenes 2 and 3 and the lower ones for Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronene 1. However, in the case of O2 adsorption the situation is completely opposite. Hence, from this point of view, Cu in the position 1 is a more suitable adsorbent site for O2 than in positions 2 and 3, while for the rest of the studied adsorbates it is vice versa. For completeness, calculated adsorption energies normalized by the number of electrons are provided in Fig. S-1 in SI. Even here, a large difference between the binding energy of O2 and the rest of adsorbed molecules is clearly visible.

The QTAIM charges at the copper and its neighboring carbon atoms of all studied systems are compiled in Table 3. One can see that the adsorption of H2O, H2O2, methanol or ethanol on the Cu-doped coronene does not affect the charge density at Cu (being around 0.66 e) while the partial charges at the Cu-binding carbons become less negative, i.e. from –0.20 to ca –0.09 e in the case of C2/C4 and from –0.30 to ca –0.25 e in the case of C9. In addition, after the adsorption of these molecules the negative charge becomes more delocalized over the adjacent carbons throughout the coronene backbone. This holds also for the adsorption of H2S, however with a slight decrease of positive charge of Cu (ca 13%), due to the lower electronegativity of sulfur in comparison to oxygen. On the other, the adsorption of O2 increases significantly the charge at Cu (from 0.66 up to 0.94 e) without large changes in the partial charges at the near carbons. Herein, the decrease of negative charge is relevant only in the case of C9, from –0.30 to –0.15 e (Table 3).

The aforementioned trends of charge densities related to Cu-doped coronene can be more or less found also in the behavior of Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 and 3. Particularly, the negative charges of two from three Cu-bonding carbons are significantly lowered (about 35–70%) after the adsorption of any of the studied molecules (see Table 3). These two carbons are C24/C3 and C11/C23 in Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 and 3, respectively. The negative charge at the remaining carbon (C12 and C25, respectively) also decreases but not as much as at the previous two, only about ca 20–25%. The positive charge at Cu is slightly increased in the case of all adsorbed molecules containing oxygen, on the contrary to Cu-doped coronene where it remains unaffected. Again, one can see the decrease in the charge at Cu after the adsorption of H2S (because of the lower electronegativity of sulfur) and the largest increase in the case of O2 adsorption (even though not as large as in the case of Cu-doped coronene; see Table 3).

However, the situation is slightly different in the case of Cu-doped circumcoronene 1. Herein, the increase in the positive charge at Cu after the adsorption is the largest among the all studied systems (being over 0.8 e for oxygen containing adsorbates). Even in the case of H2S adsorption this charge is increased from 0.67 up to 0.73 e, see Table 3. In addition, the negative charge of only one of the Cu-binding carbons (particularly C2) is lowered after the adsorption, while the charges of remaining two (C1, C11) are almost unchanged. The exception is the interaction with O2 where the negative charges of all Cu-binding carbons are decreased. One can conclude that the changes in charge densities of the studied species are closely related to the perturbations in the original six-membered ring “benzene-like” structure.

Localization of the spin densities at the studied systems without the adsorbate molecules is shown in Fig. 3 (doped coronene) and 4 (doped circumcoronenes), and drawings of the systems containing adsorbed molecules are presented in Fig. 5 (doped coronene) and 6 – 8 (doped circumcoronenes). It can be seen from Figs. 3–8 that the distribution of spin densities is only a little affected by the adsorbed molecules. In the cases of Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronene 1 and 2, the most of the spin density is naturally located at Cu and in its vicinity (Figs. 3–7). This fact holds for both the systems with and without adsorbed molecules. However, the spin density is more delocalized/scattered over the Cu-doped circumcoronene 3 backbone (cf. Figs. 4c and 8). Herein the influence of the adsorbed molecule on the spin localization is larger, centering the spin density more towards the Cu atom. Moreover, there are two special cases, the Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 and 3 plus O2 (cf. Figs. 7f and 8f, respectively), containing particularly an extremely large amount of spin at the O2 residue. The partial spin density at each of these oxygens is larger than 0.5, which leads to the negative values of spin densities at the near carbons. This can be related to a weaker Cu–O bond in systems 2 and 3 in comparison with system 1, see bond lengths (Table 1), adsorption energies (Table 2) and PDOS plots ( Fig. 9 in the following subsection). For completeness, the QTAIM spin densities at Cu and its neighboring carbons are compiled in Table S-3 in SI.

Partial density of states (PDOS) of O2 adsorbed on all studied Cu-doped surfaces in comparison with free O2 gas-phase molecule are shown in Fig. 9. In this section only the adsorption of O2 (being energetically the most preferred one from the studied adsorbents, see E
ads values in Table 2) is discussed. In the case of Cu-doped coronene and Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 there is strong coupling (hybridization) between the 5σ and 1π orbitals of O2 and Cu-d orbitals at ca –7 and ca –3eV (see Fig. 9a and b, respectively). This coupling can be responsible for the adsorption of O2 leading also to the elongation of O–O bond to 1.42Å. A similar behavior was reported by Liu and Huang for the adsorption of O2 on Pt over the N-doped graphene surface [11]. However, such coupling between O2 and Cu-d orbitals is not found in the case of Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 and 3 (see Fig. 9c and d, respectively). Herein, particular PDOSs fit each other well only in the region near the Fermi level. This points out to a weaker bond between Cu and O when comparing to the Cu-doped coronene or Cu-doped circumcoronene 1, which is also in agreement with the reported Cu–O bond distances (i.e. 1.95Å vs. 1.85Å, respectively, see Table 1) and adsorption energies (i.e. −3.2eV vs. −3.7eV, respectively, see Table 2).

DOS of all studied systems are plotted in Fig. S-2 in SI. In the case of Cu-doped coronene and Cu-doped circumcoronene 1, the DOS differs only in the case of system plus O2 (see blue dashed line in Fig. S-2a and S-2b, respectively). Apart from this case, all the rest of DOS spectra in Fig. S-2a and S-2b are similar to each other. This is because the electron distribution in these systems is only a little affected by the adsorbent molecule (aside from O2), see also QTAIM charges in Table 3. On the other hand, in the case of Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 and 3 not only DOS of system plus O2 but also DOS of system without adsorbent molecule does not fit well the rest of the DOS spectra (see black and blue dashed line Fig. S-2c and S-2 d, respectively). These differences are related to the aforementioned structural and electronic changes which occur in these systems after the adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QTAIM analysis of bond critical points
ce:section.ce:para:
Additional information on the electronic structure can be obtained from the QTAIM analysis of bond critical points. Critical points are certain points where the first derivative (gradient) of electron density vanishes i.e. ∇(ρ) = 0. Bader described four types of critical points [18], one of them being a saddle point of electron density between two atoms forming a chemical bond is called bond critical point (BCP). Besides the charge density in BCP, its Laplacian (representing the local charge concentration or depletion) and ellipticity (the measure of “double bond” or π character) are also useful parameters. In addition, delocalization index (DI) can be used to estimate the formal order of C–C bond [40]. Plots of BCP charge densities ρ BCP, Laplacians Δρ BCP, ellipticities ε BCP, and delocalization indices DI are presented in
Figs. 10–14. For completeness, all the calculated BCP values are provided in Tables S4–S7 in SI. First of all, it has to be mentioned that BCPs of all three Cu–C bonds are well defined only in the case of Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 systems (see Fig. 10). On the other hand, in the cases of Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronenes 2 and 3, only two BCPs for Cu–C bonds can be distinguished; the exception is only Cu-doped coronene plus O2with three Cu-C BCP found (cf. Figs. 11 and 12 and Tables S4–S7 in SI). Obviously this is related to the longer distances between Cu and one particular C atom (Table 1). Changes of the BCP characteristics of Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 after the adsorption have well defined trends. Charge densities ρ BCP, Laplacians Δρ BCP and delocalization indices DI in one BCP remain more or less the same in the case of Cu-C1, while in the rest two BCPs (Cu-C2 and Cu-C11) they are slightly lowered (±10–20%), see Fig. 10. Perturbations in these trends can be found only in the case of adsorption of O2, which is specific because both oxygens interact with Cu. Values of all ellipticities ε BCP are lower than 0.09 so they can be considered negligible (see Fig. 10). The situation is once again more complicated in the case of Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronenes 2 and 3. Herein, changes after the adsorption in charge densities ρ BCP, Laplacians Δρ BCP and delocalization indices DI in one BCP are not very dramatic (see Figs. 11a and 12b and Cu-C9 column in Table S-4), while, on the contrary, there is a large decrease of all these three parameters in the second BCP (see Figs. 11b and 12a and Cu-C4/Cu-C2 values in Table S-4). This decrease is related to the prolongation and “weakening” of the particular Cu–C bond as a result of the interaction with adsorbed molecule (cf. distances in Table 1). Again, the behavior of the systems with adsorbed O2 is different and does not follow the aforementioned trends. Ellipticities of the prolonged bonds become higher after the adsorption, from ca. 0.05 to ca. 0.15 in Cu-doped circumcoronene 3 (Fig. 12a) and even up to 0.40–0.71 in Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 (Fig. 11b). However the largest increases in ellipticities have been found in Cu-doped coronene (cf. Table S-4). These values, ranging from 0.79 (H2O2 adsorption) to 3.42 (methanol adsorption), indicate extremely high π character of these bonds (Cu-C4/Cu-C2).

BCP characteristics of Cu–O bonds are plotted in Fig. 13. In this case a comparison to some kind of a reference can be useful. In this respect two coordination complexes with Cu as a central atom, i.e. the Cu(H2O)6 and Cu(H2O)5 complexes are considered. In fact, given that the investigated BCP properties do not vary dramatically for the adsorption of water, hydrogen peroxide, methanol or ethanol, these two standards are sufficient for Cu–O bond study. BCP characteristics of Cu(H2O)6 and Cu(H2O)5 complexes are compiled in Table 4 and their structures with different BCPs depicted are shown in Fig. 15. Herein, abbreviations in the subscripts E and A stand for “equatorial” and “axial”, respectively, indicating the position of ligand (water molecule). In the case of Cu(H2O)5 it is also necessary to distinguish between BCPE1 and BCPE2 because these four Cu–O bonds are not equivalent as it is in the case of Cu(H2O)6 with four equivalent Cu–O bonds in the “equatorial” plane (see Fig. 15). The coordination polyhedron of Cu in Cu(H2O)6 is a distorted octahedron and in Cu(H2O)5 it is a square pyramid (The optimized geometries of these two complexes were taken from one of the authors previous work [41].). One can see by comparison between the values in Table 4 and Fig. 13 that, the BCP parameters of Cu–O bond E1 in Cu(H2O)5 corresponds well with the ones of Cu–O bond in Cu-doped coronene plus water (e.g. ρ BCP:±0.08bohr−3 or Δρ BCP:±0.51bohr−5). Similar agreement to the E1 bond in Cu(H2O)5 can be found also for the Cu-doped circumcoronene 2 and 3 (cf. data in Fig. 13c and d). On the other hand, the BCP parameters of Cu–O bond in Cu-doped circumcoronene 1 are more similar to the equatorial Cu–O bond in Cu(H2O)6 (in terms of DI:±0.35) and/or to the E2 bond in Cu(H2O)5 (in terms of Δρ BCP:±0.41bohr−5 vs. 0.44bohr−5), as can be seen in Table 4 and Fig. 13b. BCP parameters of the axial bonds of both Cu(H2O)6 as well as Cu(H2O)5 do not correspond well to the ones related to Cu–O bonds in the studied systems. Obviously, most of the BCP characteristics of Cu–S bond (in the case of H2S adsorption) are different than the ones related to the Cu-O. The difference between Cu-S and Cu-O is negligible only in the case of ρ BCP (being ca. 10%) while the Laplacians and ellipticities in Cu-S are notably lower and DIs are, on the contrary, about 20–30% higher than the ones in Cu-O. In the case of H2S adsorption, comparison to Cu(H2O)6 or Cu(H2O)5 is meaningless but there are data available in literature. Our calculated Cu-S ρ BCP and Δρ BCP of Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronenes 2 and 3 (being around 0.07bohr−3 and 0.19bohr−5, respectively) are in good agreement with the values 0.08bohr−3 and 0.20bohr−5 reported for partially covalent Cu–S bond in H2S-Cu3, H2S-Cu2Au and H2S-CuAu2 by Pakiari and Jamshidi [42].

The last one of the investigated bonds is the C–C bond formed after the adsorption of organic molecule, leading to the pulling of Cu atom far from the carbon surface. BCP characteristics of this C–C bond are shown in Fig. 14. This C–C bond does not have a character of a standard aromatic carbon bond, judging from the comparison to the C–C bond in benzene. BCP parameters of C–C bond in benzene are: ρ BCP = 0.311bohr−3, Δρ BCP = −0.877bohr−5, ε BCP = 0.198, DI(C,C) = 1.390 [43,44]. One can see from Fig. 14 that all four investigated BCP characteristics of the studied C–C bond are significantly lower or less negative in the case of Δρ BCP than the ones related to benzene. The difference is the largest in the case of Laplacian Δρ BCP. Non-zero BCP ellipticities indicate the partially preserved double bond character but interestingly, the obtained DI(C,C) values of ca. 1 correspond well to the DI of single C–C bond in ethane (being 1.01, see Refs. [43,44]).

In this work, the adsorption ability of a set of organic molecules towards the copper-doped coronene and circumcoronene were theoretically investigated combining DFT methods and QTAIM analysis. In the case of circumcoronene Cu doping was performed at three different sites, indicated as 1, 2 and 3. The most negative adsorption energies (meaning the most exothermic reaction) were obtained for O2, with energetic outcome ranging from −3.1 to −3.7eV. Nevertheless, the adsorption of all the studied organic molecules over all Cu-doped surfaces was found exothermic. In the case of Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronenes 2 and 3, the adsorbed molecule pulls the Cu from the carbon surface to such extent that it causes the formation of five-membered carbon ring within the coronene/circumcoronene backbone. This transformation does not occur only in the case of Cu-doped circumcoronene 1, which seems to contain Cu in the most stable place. From this point of view, the Cu doped-circumcoronene 1 is the most suitable model system for a Cu doped graphene even though energetically it is not the most preferred one. The partial charge densities at Cu are slightly increased after the adsorption while negative charges at the adjacent carbons became less negative. The largest changes in partial charges in the vicinity of Cu were found for the Cu-doped coronene and circumcoronene 3. According to the QTAIM analysis of BCPs, the character of the bond between Cu and O of the adsorbed molecule is similar to the one of equatorial Cu-O bond in Cu(H2O)6 and Cu(H2O)5 coordination complexes. These results show the ability of Cu-doped circumcoronenes (and also of Cu-doped graphene in general) to bind small oxygen (or sulfur) containing organic molecules by means of forming coordination bonds with them. Hence, the Cu-doped graphene materials are suitable as powerful sensors and/or adsorbents in practical applications.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work received financial support from Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia (FCT/MEC) through national funds, and co-financed by the European Union (FEDER funds) under the Partnership Agreement PT2020, through projects UID/QUI/50006/2013, POCI/01/0145/FEDER/007265, and NORTE-01-0145-FEDER-000011 (LAQV@REQUIMTE). To all financing sources the authors are greatly indebted.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://doi:10.1016/j.physe.2017.09.004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Atoms in Molecules: A Quantum Theory

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: T.A. Keith, AIMAll, version 14.04.17; TK Gristmill Software: Overland Park, KS, (aim.tkgristmill.com), 2014.
P. Flükiger, H.P. Lüthi, S. Sortmann, J. Weber, ‘MOLEKEL 4.3.’ Swiss Center for Scientific Computing: Manno, Switzerland, 2002.
